abstraction|<div class='def-intro'>The concept of hiding complex implementation details and showing only the necessary features of an object.</div><h3>Key Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Simplicity:</strong> Reduces complexity by hiding details.</li><li><strong>Security:</strong> Restricts access to internal states.</li><li><strong>Modularity:</strong> Easier to manage large codebases.</li></ul><h3>Advantages:</h3><p>Improved code maintainability and reduced cognitive load for developers.</p><h3>Real-world Uses:</h3><p>API Design, Abstract Classes in Java/C++, UI buttons (User doesn't know the logic inside).</p>
algorithm|<div class='def-intro'>A finite sequence of well-defined computer-implementable instructions to solve a problem.</div><h3>Key Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Finiteness:</strong> Must terminate after a finite number of steps.</li><li><strong>Definiteness:</strong> Each step must be precisely defined.</li><li><strong>Input/Output:</strong> Must accept inputs and produce specific results.</li><li><strong>Effectiveness:</strong> Operations must be basic enough to be performed manually.</li></ul><h3>Real-world Applications:</h3><p>Google Maps routing, E-commerce recommendations, Image processing.</p><h3>Complexity:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Time Complexity: O(f(n))</span> <span class='tag'>Space Complexity: O(g(n))</span></div>
amazon river|<div class='def-intro'>The largest river by discharge volume of water in the world, and by some measures, the longest.</div><h3>Key Statistics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Length:</strong> Approx 6,400 km (3,976 miles).</li><li><strong>Width:</strong> Up to 11 km in the dry season.</li><li><strong>Discharge:</strong> 209,000 cubic meters per second.</li></ul><h3>Ecological Significance:</h3><p>Home to 1 in 10 known species on Earth. It acts as the lifeforce for the Amazon Rainforest.</p><h3>Geographic Features:</h3><p>Flows through Peru, Brazil, Colombia, and Ecuador.</p>
android|<div class='def-intro'>A mobile operating system based on a modified version of the Linux kernel and other open-source software.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Customizability:</strong> Users can change skins, icons, and launchers.</li><li><strong>App Ecosystem:</strong> Managed via Google Play Store.</li><li><strong>Open Source:</strong> Developers can modify the source code (AOSP).</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>Founded by Andy Rubin in 2003, acquired by Google in 2005.</p><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Powers over 3 billion active devices worldwide.</p>
angular|<div class='def-intro'>A TypeScript-based open-source web application framework led by Google.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Two-way Data Binding:</strong> Automatic synchronization between Model and View.</li><li><strong>Dependency Injection:</strong> Increases modularity and testability.</li><li><strong>Directives:</strong> Extend HTML with custom behaviors.</li></ul><h3>Pros/Cons:</h3><ul><li><strong>Pros:</strong> Robust environment, high performance for enterprise apps.</li><li><strong>Cons:</strong> Steep learning curve, verbose syntax.</li></ul><h3>Real-world Uses:</h3><p>Gmail, Forbes, PayPal, Upwork.</p>
api|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Application Programming Interface</strong>. A set of protocols and tools for building software applications.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>REST:</strong> Representational State Transfer (Stateless).</li><li><strong>SOAP:</strong> Simple Object Access Protocol (Strict).</li><li><strong>GraphQL:</strong> Allows clients to request exactly what they need.</li></ul><h3>Key Benefits:</h3><p>Abstraction, Reusability, Decoupling of services.</p><h3>Examples:</h3><p>Google Maps API, Stripe API, Twitter API.</p>
application|<div class='def-intro'>Software designed to perform a specific group of coordinated functions, tasks, or activities.</div><h3>Categories:</h3><ul><li><strong>Web Apps:</strong> Accessed via browser (Notion).</li><li><strong>Desktop Apps:</strong> Installed on OS (Visual Studio).</li><li><strong>Mobile Apps:</strong> Built for iOS/Android (Instagram).</li></ul><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Interface -> Processing Logic -> Data Storage.</p>
array|<div class='def-intro'>A data structure consisting of a collection of elements, each identified by at least one array index.</div><h3>Key Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Contiguous Memory:</strong> Elements are stored in adjacent memory locations.</li><li><strong>Random Access:</strong> O(1) time to access any element by index.</li><li><strong>Homogeneity:</strong> All elements must be of the same data type.</li></ul><h3>Complexity Analysis:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Access: O(1)</span> <span class='tag'>Search: O(N)</span> <span class='tag'>Insertion: O(N)</span></div>
artificial intelligence|<div class='def-intro'>Simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems.</div><h3>Major Subfields:</h3><ul><li><strong>Machine Learning:</strong> Statistical methods to learn from data.</li><li><strong>Natural Language Processing (NLP):</strong> Understanding human speech/text.</li><li><strong>Computer Vision:</strong> Interpreting visual data from the world.</li></ul><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Revolutionizing healthcare, finance, and creative industries.</p>
assembly language|<div class='def-intro'>Any low-level programming language in which there is a very strong correspondence between the instructions and the architecture's machine code.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Mnemonics:</strong> Short codes like `MOV`, `ADD`, `PUSH`.</li><li><strong>Direct Memory Access:</strong> Ultimate control over hardware.</li><li><strong>High Performance:</strong> Extremely fast execution.</li></ul><h3>Uses:</h3><p>Bootloaders, OS Kernels, Reverse Engineering, IoT.</p>
asymptotic analysis|<div class='def-intro'>Method for calculating the efficiency of an algorithm by disregarding constant factors and small input sizes.</div><h3>Core Notations:</h3><ul><li><strong>Big O:</strong> Upper bound (Worst case).</li><li><strong>Omega:</strong> Lower bound (Best case).</li><li><strong>Theta:</strong> Average bound.</li></ul><h3>Why it matters:</h3><p>Helps engineers predict scalability before writing code.</p>
atom|<div class='def-intro'>The basic building block of chemistry. It is the smallest unit into which matter can be divided without the release of electrically charged particles.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><ul><li><strong>Protons:</strong> Positively charged, in the nucleus.</li><li><strong>Neutrons:</strong> Neutrally charged, in the nucleus.</li><li><strong>Electrons:</strong> Negatively charged, orbiting the nucleus.</li></ul><h3>Properties:</h3><p>Defined by the Atomic Number (number of protons).</p>
avl tree|<div class='def-intro'>A self-balancing binary search tree where the difference between heights of left and right subtrees cannot be more than one.</div><h3>Balancing Mechanism:</h3><p>Rotations (Left, Right, Left-Right, Right-Left) are performed after insertion/deletion.</p><h3>Performance:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Search: O(log N)</span> <span class='tag'>Insert: O(log N)</span></div><h3>Uses:</h3><p>Databases where lookups are frequent.</p>
aws|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Amazon Web Services</strong>. A comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon.</div><h3>Core Services:</h3><ul><li><strong>EC2:</strong> Scalable virtual servers.</li><li><strong>S3:</strong> Highly available object storage.</li><li><strong>Lambda:</strong> Event-driven serverless computing.</li></ul><h3>Global Impact:</h3><p>Powers a significant portion of the internet (Netflix, Twitch, LinkedIn).</p>
backtracking|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithmic technique for solving problems recursively by trying to build a solution incrementally, and removing those solutions that fail to satisfy the constraints.</div><h3>Key Concept:</h3><p>Depth-First Search (DFS) with a pruning mechanism.</p><h3>Famous Problems:</h3><ul><li><strong>N-Queens:</strong> Placing queens on a chessboard.</li><li><strong>Sudoku:</strong> Filling a grid without repetition.</li><li><strong>Maze Solver:</strong> Finding a path to the exit.</li></ul>
bandwidth|<div class='def-intro'>The maximum amount of data transmitted over an internet connection in a given amount of time.</div><h3>Measurement:</h3><p>Usually measured in Megabits per second (Mbps) or Gigabits per second (Gbps).</p><h3>Factors:</h3><ul><li><strong>Latency:</strong> Delay in transmission.</li><li><strong>Throughput:</strong> Actual speed achieved.</li></ul>
big o notation|<div class='def-intro'>Mathematical notation used to describe the efficiency and performance of an algorithm.</div><h3>Common Big O Ranks:</h3><ul><li><strong>O(1):</strong> Constant time (Fastest).</li><li><strong>O(log N):</strong> Logarithmic (Efficient).</li><li><strong>O(N):</strong> Linear.</li><li><strong>O(N log N):</strong> Quasilinear (Sorting).</li><li><strong>O(N^2):</strong> Quadratic (Slow).</li></ul><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Essential for technical interviews and system optimization.</p>
binary|<div class='def-intro'>A base-2 numbering system that uses only two digits: 0 and 1.</div><h3>How it works:</h3><p>Information is represented by states of electrical signals (On/Off).</p><h3>Importance:</h3><p>The fundamental language of all modern computers.</p>
binary search|<div class='def-intro'>A fast search algorithm with O(log N) complexity, used on sorted collections.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Repeatedly divides the search interval in half. If the target is less than the middle element, search the lower half; otherwise, search the upper half.</p><h3>Pre-requisite:</h3><p>The data <strong>MUST</strong> be sorted.</p>
binary search tree|<div class='def-intro'>A node-based binary tree data structure which has the property that left child < parent < right child.</div><h3>Operations:</h3><ul><li><strong>Search:</strong> log N.</li><li><strong>In-order Traversal:</strong> Produces sorted output.</li></ul><h3>Applications:</h3><p>Symbol tables, Routing protocols.</p>
binary tree|<div class='def-intro'>A tree data structure in which each node has at most two children.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Full:</strong> Nodes have 0 or 2 children.</li><li><strong>Perfect:</strong> All leaves are at the same level.</li><li><strong>Balanced:</strong> Height difference is minimal.</li></ul>
biology|<div class='def-intro'>The scientific study of life and living organisms.</div><h3>Main Disciplines:</h3><ul><li><strong>Genetics:</strong> Study of genes and heredity.</li><li><strong>Cell Biology:</strong> Study of structural units of life.</li><li><strong>Ecology:</strong> Study of organisms interacting with their environment.</li></ul><h3>Goal:</h3><p>Understanding the complexity of life from atoms to ecosystems.</p>
bitcoin|<div class='def-intro'>The world's first decentralized digital currency, introduced in 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Blockchain:</strong> Public ledger of all transactions.</li><li><strong>Proof of Work:</strong> Consensus mechanism (Mining).</li><li><strong>Scarcity:</strong> Capped at 21 million BTC.</li></ul>
bit manipulation|<div class='def-intro'>The act of algorithmically manipulating bits or other pieces of data shorter than a word.</div><h3>Operators:</h3><ul><li><strong>AND (`&`):</strong> Sets bit to 1 if both bits are 1.</li><li><strong>OR (`|`):</strong> Sets bit to 1 if one bit is 1.</li><li><strong>XOR (`^`):</strong> Sets bit to 1 if only one bit is 1.</li><li><strong>NOT (`~`):</strong> Inverts all bits.</li></ul>
black hole|<div class='def-intro'>A place in space where gravity pulls so much that even light cannot get out.</div><h3>Key Zones:</h3><ul><li><strong>Event Horizon:</strong> The boundary of no escape.</li><li><strong>Singularity:</strong> The core of infinite density.</li></ul><h3>Discovery:</h3><p>Predicted by Einstein's Theory of General Relativity.</p>
blockchain|<div class='def-intro'>A system of recording information in a way that makes it difficult or impossible to change, hack, or cheat the system.</div><h3>Architecture:</h3><ul><li><strong>Block:</strong> Contains data and hash of previous block.</li><li><strong>Network:</strong> Peer-to-peer verification.</li></ul><h3>Beyond Crypto:</h3><p>Voting systems, Supply chain tracking, Medical records.</p>
boolean|<div class='def-intro'>A data type that has one of two possible values: True or False.</div><h3>Logic Gates:</h3><p>AND, OR, NOT gates form the basis of CPU logic.</p><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Conditionals (e.g., `if (isAlive) { ... }`).</p>
breadth first search|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures level by level.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Starts at the root and explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth before moving to the next level.</p><h3>Data Structure:</h3><p>Uses a <strong>Queue</strong> (FIFO).</p>
bubble sort|<div class='def-intro'>A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.</div><h3>Pros/Cons:</h3><ul><li><strong>Pros:</strong> Easy to implement.</li><li><strong>Cons:</strong> Very slow (O(N^2)).</li></ul><h3>Status:</h3><p>Rarely used in production, mostly for teaching.</p>
byte|<div class='def-intro'>A unit of digital information that most commonly consists of eight bits.</div><h3>Capacity:</h3><p>Can store a number between 0 and 255. One byte can store one ASCII character.</p><h3>Scaling:</h3><p>KB (1024 bytes), MB (1024 KB), GB (1024 MB).</p>
c|<div class='def-intro'>A powerful general-purpose programming language developed in 1972 at Bell Labs.</div><h3>Legacy:</h3><p>The "Mother" of modern languages (Syntax used in C++, Java, JS, C#).</p><h3>Core Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Pointer arithmetic.</strong></li><li><strong>Close to hardware.</strong></li><li><strong>No Garbage Collection.</strong></li></ul>
c++|<div class='def-intro'>A general-purpose programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup as an extension of C.</div><h3>Core Pillars:</h3><ul><li><strong>Performance:</strong> Compiled to machine code.</li><li><strong>OOP:</strong> Classes, Inheritance, Polymorphism.</li><li><strong>Generic Programming:</strong> STL Templates.</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>Released in 1985, originally called "C with Classes".</p>
cache|<div class='def-intro'>A hardware or software component that stores data so that future requests for that data can be served faster.</div><h3>Locality of Reference:</h3><ul><li><strong>Temporal:</strong> Accessing same data again soon.</li><li><strong>Spatial:</strong> Accessing nearby data.</li></ul><h3>Levels:</h3><p>L1 (Fastest), L2, L3 (Largest) in CPUs.</p>
calculus|<div class='def-intro'>The mathematical study of continuous change, in the same way that geometry is the study of shape.</div><h3>Main Concepts:</h3><ul><li><strong>Differential:</strong> Rates of change (Derivatives).</li><li><strong>Integral:</strong> Accumulation of quantities (Areas).</li></ul><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Physics, Economics, Engineering, Data Science.</p>
chemistry|<div class='def-intro'>Scientific study of the properties and behavior of matter.</div><h3>Key Focus:</h3><p>How atoms bond to form molecules and how they react with one another.</p><h3>Significance:</h3><p>Known as the "Central Science" connecting physics and biology.</p>
class|<div class='def-intro'>An extensible program-code-template for creating objects in Object-Oriented Programming.</div><h3>Components:</h3><ul><li><strong>Data Members:</strong> Variables (State).</li><li><strong>Member Functions:</strong> Methods (Behavior).</li><li><strong>Access Specifiers:</strong> Private, Public, Protected.</li></ul>
cloud computing|<div class='def-intro'>The on-demand availability of computer system resources, especially data storage and computing power.</div><h3>Models:</h3><ul><li><strong>SaaS:</strong> Software as a Service (Google Docs).</li><li><strong>PaaS:</strong> Platform as a Service (Heroku).</li><li><strong>IaaS:</strong> Infrastructure as a Service (Azure/AWS).</li></ul>
compiler|<div class='def-intro'>A special program that translates a programming language's source code into machine code, bytecode, or another language.</div><h3>Pipeline:</h3><p>Scanning -> Parsing -> Semantic Analysis -> Optimization -> Code Generation.</p><h3>Examples:</h3><p>GCC (GNU), Clang, MSVC.</p>
complexity class|<div class='def-intro'>A set of problems with related complexity-based requirements.</div><h3>Major Classes:</h3><ul><li><strong>P:</strong> Solvable in polynomial time.</li><li><strong>NP:</strong> Verifiable in polynomial time.</li><li><strong>NP-Hard:</strong> At least as hard as the hardest problems in NP.</li></ul>
computer|<div class='def-intro'>An electronic device for storing and processing data, typically in binary form.</div><h3>Evolution:</h3><p>Vacuum Tubes -> Transistors -> Integrated Circuits -> Microprocessors.</p><h3>Essential Components:</h3><p>ALU, Control Unit, Registers, RAM.</p>
computer vision|<div class='def-intro'>A field of AI that enables computers and systems to derive meaningful information from digital images and videos.</div><h3>Techniques:</h3><ul><li><strong>Object Detection:</strong> Identifying items (cars, pedestrians).</li><li><strong>Image Segmentation:</strong> Partitioning image into parts.</li></ul><h3>Devices:</h3><p>Self-driving cars, FaceID scanners.</p>
constructor|<div class='def-intro'>A special member function of a class that is executed whenever we create new objects of that class.</div><h3>Rules:</h3><ul><li><strong>Same Name:</strong> Must match class name.</li><li><strong>No Return Type:</strong> Not even void.</li></ul><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Ensures that every object starts with a valid state.</p>
cpu|<div class='def-intro'>The primary component of a computer that acts as its "control center."</div><h3>Function Cycle:</h3><ol><li><strong>Fetch:</strong> Get instruction from RAM.</li><li><strong>Decode:</strong> Figure out what to do.</li><li><strong>Execute:</strong> Perform the math/move.</li></ol><h3>Performance:</h3><p>Measured in Clock Speed (GHz) and Number of Cores.</p>
cryptocurrency|<div class='def-intro'>A digital or virtual currency that is secured by cryptography, making it nearly impossible to counterfeit.</div><h3>Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Distributed Ledger:</strong> Shared across nodes.</li><li><strong>Transparency:</strong> Public transaction history.</li><li><strong>Anonymity:</strong> Users identified by addresses, not names.</li></ul>
css|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Cascading Style Sheets</strong>. Language used for describing the presentation of a document written in HTML.</div><h3>Evolution:</h3><ul><li><strong>CSS3:</strong> Added Flexbox, Grid, Animations, Shadows.</li><li><strong>SASS/LESS:</strong> Preprocessors for cleaner code.</li></ul><h3>Role:</h3><p>The "Skin" and "Beauty" of the web.</p>
cybersecurity|<div class='def-intro'>The practice of protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks.</div><h3>Threats:</h3><ul><li><strong>Ransomware:</strong> Locking files for money.</li><li><strong>Social Engineering:</strong> Decieving people for passwords.</li></ul><h3>Defense:</h3><p>Encryption, Firewalls, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA).</p>
database|<div class='def-intro'>An organized collection of structured information, or data, typically stored electronically in a computer system.</div><h3>Management Systems (DBMS):</h3><ul><li><strong>Relational:</strong> SQL-based (MySQL, PostgreSQL).</li><li><strong>Document:</strong> JSON-like (MongoDB).</li><li><strong>Graph:</strong> Relationship-focused (Neo4j).</li></ul>
data science|<div class='def-intro'>An interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms and systems to extract knowledge from data.</div><h3>Technologies:</h3><ul><li><strong>Python/R:</strong> Main languages.</li><li><strong>Pandas/NumPy:</strong> Data cleaning.</li><li><strong>Matplotlib/Seaborn:</strong> Visualization.</li></ul>
data structure|<div class='def-intro'>A data organization, management, and storage format that enables efficient access and modification.</div><h3>Categories:</h3><ul><li><strong>Linear:</strong> Arrays, Stacks, Queues, Linked Lists.</li><li><strong>Non-Linear:</strong> Trees, Graphs.</li></ul><h3>Optimization:</h3><p>Right choice can reduce an app's time from minutes to milliseconds.</p>
deep learning|<div class='def-intro'>A subfield of machine learning based on artificial neural networks with representation learning.</div><h3>Why "Deep"?</h3><p>Refers to the use of many layers (Input, Hidden, Output) in the network.</p><h3>Achievements:</h3><p>AlphaGo, Real-time translation, Stable Diffusion.</p>
democracy|<div class='def-intro'>A system of government by the whole population or all the eligible members of a state, typically through elected representatives.</div><h3>Principles:</h3><ul><li><strong>Rule of Law:</strong> Everyone follows the same laws.</li><li><strong>Human Rights:</strong> Freedom of speech, assembly.</li></ul>
depth first search|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures by going deep into a branch before backtracking.</div><h3>Data Structure:</h3><p>Uses a <strong>Stack</strong> (FILO) or Recursion.</p><h3>Worst Case:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>O(V + E)</span></div>
devops|<div class='def-intro'>A set of practices that combines software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops).</div><h3>Core Concept:</h3><p>CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment).</p><h3>Tools:</h3><p>Git, Jenkins, Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform.</p>
dictionary|<div class='def-intro'>A collection of keys and values, where each key is mapped to a value.</div><h3>Performance:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Lookup: O(1)</span></div><h3>Aliases:</h3><p>Map (Java), Object (JS), Associative Array (PHP).</p>
dijkstra algorithm|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph.</div><h3>Constraint:</h3><p>Does not work with negative edge weights (Use Bellman-Ford for that).</p><h3>Implementation:</h3><p>Often uses a <strong>Priority Queue</strong> for efficiency.</p>
distributed system|<div class='def-intro'>A computing environment in which various components are spread across multiple computers on a network.</div><h3>Challenges:</h3><ul><li><strong>Consistency:</strong> All data must be the same everywhere.</li><li><strong>Availability:</strong> System must always be up.</li><li><strong>Fault Tolerance:</strong> System must survive machine crashes.</li></ul>
django|<div class='def-intro'>A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design.</div><h3>Architecture:</h3><p>MTV (Model-Template-View).</p><h3>Philosophy:</h3><p>Includes built-in Admin, Auth, and DB migrations.</p>
dna|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Deoxyribonucleic Acid</strong>. The molecule that carries genetic information for the development and functioning of an organism.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><p>Double Helix discovered by Watson and Crick (and Rosalind Franklin).</p><h3>Building Blocks:</h3><p>Nucleotides (Adenine, Thymine, Cytosine, Guanine).</p>
docker|<div class='def-intro'>A set of platform as a service products that use OS-level virtualization to deliver software in packages called containers.</div><h3>Key Benefit:</h3><p>Ensures environment consistency across development, testing, and production.</p><h3>Metaphor:</h3><p>Like a shipping container for your code.</p>
domain name system|<div class='def-intro'>The phonebook of the Internet. Humans access information online through domain names, like google.com.</div><h3>Process:</h3><ol><li><strong>Request:</strong> Browser asks DNS for IP.</li><li><strong>Resolution:</strong> DNS returns IP like `142.250.190.46`.</li><li><strong>Connection:</strong> Browser connects to server.</li></ol>
dynamic programming|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithmic technique for solving optimization problems by breaking them down into simpler subproblems and storing the results.</div><h3>Key Steps:</h3><ul><li><strong>Memoization:</strong> Storing results of expensive function calls (Top-down).</li><li><strong>Tabulation:</strong> Filling a table (Bottom-up).</li></ul><h3>Classical Problems:</h3><p>Fibonacci, Longest Common Subsequence, Knapsack.</p>
earth|<div class='def-intro'>Our home planet. It is the only planet in the solar system known to harbor life.</div><h3>Dynamic Nature:</h3><ul><li><strong>Atmosphere:</strong> 78% Nitrogen, 21% Oxygen.</li><li><strong>Layers:</strong> Crust, Mantle, Outer Core, Inner Core.</li><li><strong>Protection:</strong> Magnetic field blocks solar radiation.</li></ul>
economics|<div class='def-intro'>The social science that studies the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services.</div><h3>Main Concepts:</h3><ul><li><strong>Opportunity Cost:</strong> Value of the next best alternative.</li><li><strong>Inflation:</strong> General increase in prices.</li><li><strong>GDP:</strong> Total value of goods produced.</li></ul>
electricity|<div class='def-intro'>The set of physical phenomena associated with the presence and motion of matter that has a property of electric charge.</div><h3>Safety:</h3><p>Conductors allow flow (Copper); Insulators block flow (Rubber).</p><h3>Impact:</h3><p>The foundation of the Second Industrial Revolution.</p>
electron|<div class='def-intro'>A stable subatomic particle with a charge of negative electricity, found in all atoms.</div><h3>Discovery:</h3><p>J.J. Thomson in 1897.</p><h3>Behavior:</h3><p>Exist in "probability clouds" rather than fixed orbits.</p>
encapsulation|<div class='def-intro'>The bundling of data with the methods that operate on that data, or the restricting of direct access to some of an object's components.</div><h3>Implementation:</h3><p>Usually achieved by making data members `private` and providing `public` methods (getters/setters).</p><h3>Benefit:</h3><p>Protects data from accidental corruption.</p>
encryption|<div class='def-intro'>The process of converting information or data into a code, especially to prevent unauthorized access.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Symmetric:</strong> Same key for lock and unlock (AES).</li><li><strong>Asymmetric:</strong> Public key to lock, Private key to unlock (RSA).</li></ul>
energy|<div class='def-intro'>The quantitative property that is transferred to a body or to a physical system, recognizable in the performance of work and in the form of heat and light.</div><h3>Forms:</h3><p>Kinetic, Potential, Thermal, Chemical, Electrical, Nuclear.</p>
environment|<div class='def-intro'>The surroundings or conditions in which a person, animal, or plant lives or operates.</div><h3>Current Status:</h3><p>Facing challenges like global warming, plastic pollution, and loss of biodiversity.</p>
evolution|<div class='def-intro'>The change in the heritable characteristics of biological populations over successive generations.</div><h3>Evidence:</h3><p>Fossil records, Homologous structures, DNA comparisons.</p>
fibonacci sequence|<div class='def-intro'>A series of numbers in which each number is the sum of the two preceding ones.</div><h3>Sequence:</h3><p>0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ...</p><h3>Applications:</h3><p>Nature (Spiral shells), Algorithms (DP), Financial markets (Fibonacci Retracement).</p>
firewall|<div class='def-intro'>A network security device that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Packet Filtering:</strong> Simplest level.</li><li><strong>Proxy Firewall:</strong> Gates at the application level.</li><li><strong>Next-Gen Firewall:</strong> Deep packet inspection.</li></ul>
flutter|<div class='def-intro'>Google's UI library to build native applications for mobile, web, and desktop from a single codebase.</div><h3>Engine:</h3><p>Uses the Skia Graphics Engine to draw every pixel manually.</p><h3>Language:</h3><p>Written in <strong>Dart</strong>.</p>
for loop|<div class='def-intro'>A control flow statement for specifying iteration, which allows code to be executed repeatedly.</div><h3>Anatomy:</h3><p>`for(int i=0; i<n; i++)` -> Initialize; Check; Update.</p><h3>Use Case:</h3><p>Iterating over elements of an array or string.</p>
frontend|<div class='def-intro'>The part of a website or app that users interact with directly.</div><h3>Key Roles:</h3><ul><li><strong>Developer:</strong> Writes HTML/CSS/JS.</li><li><strong>Designer:</strong> Creates layout and assets.</li></ul><h3>Popular Frameworks:</h3><p>React, Vue, Angular, Next.js.</p>
function|<div class='def-intro'>In computer programming, a function is a sequence of program instructions that performs a specific task, packaged as a unit.</div><h3>Terminologies:</h3><ul><li><strong>Call:</strong> Triggering the function.</li><li><strong>Parameter:</strong> Placeholders in definition.</li><li><strong>Argument:</strong> Actual values passed.</li></ul>
galaxy|<div class='def-intro'>A gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, interstellar gas, dust, and dark matter.</div><h3>Known Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Elliptical:</strong> Rounded shapes, older stars.</li><li><strong>Spiral:</strong> Disk-shaped with arms (Milky Way).</li><li><strong>Irregular:</strong> No specific structure.</li></ul><h3>Scale:</h3><p>Typical galaxies contain between 10 million and 1 trillion stars.</p>
game theory|<div class='def-intro'>The study of mathematical models of strategic interaction among rational agents.</div><h3>Key Concepts:</h3><ul><li><strong>Nash Equilibrium:</strong> No player can benefit by changing strategy alone.</li><li><strong>Zero-Sum Game:</strong> One's gain is another's loss.</li></ul><h3>Applications:</h3><p>Economics, Evolutionary Biology, Computer Science, Global Politics.</p>
garbage collection|<div class='def-intro'>A form of automatic memory management where the garbage collector attempts to reclaim memory occupied by objects that are no longer in use.</div><h3>Mechanisms:</h3><ul><li><strong>Reference Counting:</strong> (Python).</li><li><strong>Mark-and-Sweep:</strong> (Java).</li></ul><h3>Pros/Cons:</h3><ul><li><strong>Pros:</strong> Prevents memory leaks.</li><li><strong>Cons:</strong> Can cause "stop-the-world" latency.</li></ul>
genetic algorithm|<div class='def-intro'>A search heuristic that is inspired by Charles Darwin's theory of natural evolution.</div><h3>Phases:</h3><ol><li><strong>Evaluation:</strong> Calculate fitness.</li><li><strong>Selection:</strong> Choose best parents.</li><li><strong>Crossover:</strong> Combine genes.</li><li><strong>Mutation:</strong> Small random change.</li></ol><h3>Uses:</h3><p>Antenna design, Logistics, Neural network architecture search.</p>
geography|<div class='def-intro'>The study of places and the relationships between people and their environments.</div><h3>Branches:</h3><ul><li><strong>Physical:</strong> Landforms, climate, water.</li><li><strong>Human:</strong> Cultures, economies, populations.</li></ul><h3>Tools:</h3><p>GPS, GIS (Geographic Information Systems), Cartography.</p>
git|<div class='def-intro'>A distributed version control system for tracking changes in source code during software development.</div><h3>Key Workflow:</h3><p>dd -> commit -> push -> pull.</p><h3>Why it's great:</h3><p>Allows multiple developers to work on the same file without overwriting each other (using branching).</p>
github|<div class='def-intro'>A provider of Internet hosting for software development and version control using Git.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Pull Requests:</strong> Code review and discussion.</li><li><strong>Actions:</strong> Automated testing and deployment.</li><li><strong>Issues:</strong> Task tracking.</li></ul><h3>Acquisition:</h3><p>Bought by Microsoft in 2018 for .5 billion.</p>
go|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source programming language designed at Google to make it easy to build simple, reliable, and efficient software.</div><h3>Strengths:</h3><ul><li><strong>Concurrency:</strong> Native support via Goroutines.</li><li><strong>Speed:</strong> Compiles to machine code.</li><li><strong>Simplicity:</strong> Minimalist syntax focused on productivity.</li></ul><h3>Popular Repos:</h3><p>Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform.</p>
google|<div class='def-intro'>An American multinational technology company that focuses on artificial intelligence, search engine, online advertising, and more.</div><h3>Flagship Products:</h3><ul><li><strong>Search:</strong> 90%+ market share.</li><li><strong>Android:</strong> Most popular mobile OS.</li><li><strong>YouTube:</strong> Leading video platform.</li></ul><h3>Mission:</h3><p>To organize the world's information and make it universally accessible.</p>
gpu|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Graphics Processing Unit</strong>. A specialized electronic circuit designed to rapidly manipulate and alter memory to accelerate the creation of images.</div><h3>Vs CPU:</h3><p>CPUs have few cores for complex tasks; GPUs have thousands of cores for repetitive mathematical tasks.</p><h3>Modern Uses:</h3><p>Gaming, Machine Learning, Crypto Mining, Scientific Simulations.</p>
graph|<div class='def-intro'>A non-linear data structure consisting of a finite set of vertices (nodes) and set of edges which connect them.</div><h3>Representations:</h3><ul><li><strong>Adjacency Matrix:</strong> 2D array [V][V].</li><li><strong>Adjacency List:</strong> Array of linked lists.</li></ul><h3>Real-world Examples:</h3><p>Social networks (Users = Nodes, Friends = Edges).</p>
gravity|<div class='def-intro'>A natural phenomenon by which all things with mass or energy are brought toward one another.</div><h3>Discovery:</h3><p>Formalized by Newton (1687) and refined by Einstein (1915).</p><h3>Role:</h3><p>Keeps the moon in orbit around Earth and planets around the Sun.</p>
greedy algorithm|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithmic paradigm that follows the problem-solving heuristic of making the locally optimal choice at each stage with the intent of finding a global optimum.</div><h3>Constraint:</h3><p>Doesn't always yield the best global solution (unlike DP).</p><h3>Classical Algorithms:</h3><p>Prim's, Kruskal's, Dijkstra, Huffman Coding, Fractional Knapsack.</p>
hash table|<div class='def-intro'>A data structure that implements an associative array abstract data type, a structure that can map keys to values.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Uses a <strong>Hash Function</strong> to compute an index into an array of buckets.</p><h3>Collision Handling:</h3><ul><li><strong>Chaining:</strong> Linked lists in buckets.</li><li><strong>Open Addressing:</strong> Finding the next empty slot.</li></ul>
heap|<div class='def-intro'>A specialized tree-based data structure which is essentially a complete tree that satisfies the heap property.</div><h3>Properties:</h3><ul><li><strong>Max-Heap:</strong> Parent node is greater than children.</li><li><strong>Min-Heap:</strong> Parent node is smaller than children.</li></ul><h3>Complexity:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Get Min/Max: O(1)</span> <span class='tag'>Insert: O(log N)</span></div>
history|<div class='def-intro'>The study of the past as it is described in written documents.</div><h3>Major Eras:</h3><p>Prehistory -> Ancient -> Middle Ages -> Modern -> Contemporary.</p><h3>Importance:</h3><p>Helps us understand why the world looks the way it does today.</p>
html|<div class='def-intro'><strong>HyperText Markup Language</strong>. The standard markup language for documents designed to be displayed in a web browser.</div><h3>Latest Version:</h3><p><strong>HTML5</strong>, which supports Video, Audio, and Canvas natively.</p><h3>Anatomy:</h3><p>Tags like <section>, <article>, and <footer> provide semantic meaning.</p>
http|<div class='def-intro'><strong>HyperText Transfer Protocol</strong>. An application-layer protocol for transmitting hypermedia documents.</div><h3>Versions:</h3><ul><li><strong>HTTP/1.1:</strong> Standard persistent connections.</li><li><strong>HTTP/2:</strong> Multiplexing (faster).</li><li><strong>HTTP/3:</strong> Based on QUIC (UDP).</li></ul>
https|<div class='def-intro'><strong>HTTP Secure</strong>. An extension of HTTP that uses TLS/SSL to encrypt communications.</div><h3>Why use it?</h3><ul><li><strong>Privacy:</strong> Hide data from sniffers.</li><li><strong>Integrity:</strong> Ensure data isn't tampered with.</li><li><strong>Authentication:</strong> Confirm server identity.</li></ul>
ide|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Integrated Development Environment</strong>. A software application that provides comprehensive facilities to computer programmers for software development.</div><h3>Common Components:</h3><ul><li><strong>Editor:</strong> For writing code.</li><li><strong>Debugger:</strong> For testing.</li><li><strong>Build Tools:</strong> For automation.</li></ul><h3>Popular IDEs:</h3><p>IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, Xcode, Visual Studio.</p>
if statement|<div class='def-intro'>A conditional statement that executes a block of code if a specific condition is true.</div><h3>Syntax (C++):</h3><p>if (condition) { // do this } else { // do that }</p><h3>Logic:</h3><p>Forms the basis of decision-making in software.</p>
inheritance|<div class='def-intro'>A mechanism in which one class acquires the property of another class.</div><h3>Benefits:</h3><ul><li><strong>Code Reusability:</strong> Share logic between classes.</li><li><strong>Polymorphism:</strong> Use base pointers for child objects.</li></ul><h3>Relationship:</h3><p>Known as an <strong>"Is-A"</strong> relationship (e.g., Dog Is-A Animal).</p>
integer|<div class='def-intro'>A datum of integral data type, a data type that represents some range of mathematical integers.</div><h3>Range (32-bit):</h3><p>-2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.</p><h3>Sign:</h3><ul><li><strong>Signed:</strong> Can be negative.</li><li><strong>Unsigned:</strong> Positive only (Double the max range).</li></ul>
interface|<div class='def-intro'>A programming construct that defines a contract for what a class must do, without defining how.</div><h3>Key Feature:</h3><p>Contains only method signatures (no implementation).</p><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Critical for decoupling code and implementing Polymorphism.</p>
internet|<div class='def-intro'>The global system of interconnected computer networks that use the Internet protocol suite (TCP/IP).</div><h3>Scale:</h3><p>Connecting over 5 billion people worldwide.</p><h3>Predecessor:</h3><p>ARPANET (1969).</p>
internet of things|<div class='def-intro'>Describes physical objects with sensors, processing ability, and software that connect and exchange data over the internet.</div><h3>Examples:</h3><p>Smart watches, Amazon Alexa, Connected cars, Industrial sensors.</p>
interpreter|<div class='def-intro'>A computer program that directly executes instructions written in a programming or scripting language, without requiring them previously to have been compiled.</div><h3>Vs Compiler:</h3><p>Interpreters are usually slower but offer more flexibility and cross-platform ease (e.g., Python, Ruby).</p>
ip address|<div class='def-intro'>A numerical label assigned to each device connected to a computer network that uses the Internet Protocol for communication.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>IPv4:</strong> 32-bit (Ex: 8.8.8.8) - Running out of space.</li><li><strong>IPv6:</strong> 128-bit (Ex: 2001:4860:4860::8888) - Infinite addresses.</li></ul>
java|<div class='def-intro'>A high-level, class-based, object-oriented programming language designed to have as few implementation dependencies as possible.</div><h3>Motto:</h3><p>"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA).</p><h3>Key Tech:</h3><p><strong>JVM (Java Virtual Machine)</strong> makes it cross-platform by running Bytecode.</p><h3>Uses:</h3><p>Banking systems, Android apps (Historically), Enterprise servers.</p>
javascript|<div class='def-intro'>A high-level, often just-in-time compiled language that is one of the core technologies of the World Wide Web.</div><h3>Growth:</h3><p>Started as a browser script; now used for servers (Node.js), Mobile (React Native), and VR.</p><h3>Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Dynamic Typing:</strong> Variables can change types.</li><li><strong>Async:</strong> Event-loop based concurrency.</li></ul>
json|<div class='def-intro'><strong>JavaScript Object Notation</strong>. A lightweight data-interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write.</div><h3>Format:</h3><p>{"key": "value", "list": [1, 2, 3]}</p><h3>Success:</h3><p>Replaced XML as the dominant format for data transfer between Client and Server.</p>
jupiter|<div class='def-intro'>The fifth planet from the Sun and the largest in the Solar System.</div><h3>Statistics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Mass:</strong> 2.5 times that of all other planets combined.</li><li><strong>Moons:</strong> 95+ (including Ganymede, the largest in the solar system).</li><li><strong>Gravity:</strong> 2.4x Earth's gravity.</li></ul>
kernel|<div class='def-intro'>A computer program at the core of a computer's operating system with complete control over everything in the system.</div><h3>Responsibilities:</h3><ul><li><strong>Process Mgmt:</strong> Who gets to use the CPU.</li><li><strong>Memory Mgmt:</strong> Who gets RAM.</li><li><strong>I/O Mgmt:</strong> Access to keyboard, disk, network.</li></ul>
kotlin|<div class='def-intro'>A cross-platform, statically typed programming language with type inference, designed to interoperate fully with Java.</div><h3>Recognition:</h3><p>Announced as the preferred language for Android development by Google in 2017.</p><h3>Improvements over Java:</h3><p>Null safety, concise syntax, Coroutines.</p>
kubernetes|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source container-orchestration system for automating computer application deployment, scaling, and management.</div><h3>Origin:</h3><p>Built by Google engineers (Project Borg) and donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation.</p><h3>Role:</h3><p>The "OS" of the cloud.</p>
lambda calculus|<div class='def-intro'>A mathematical system in mathematical logic for expressing computation based on function abstraction and application.</div><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Directly inspired functional programming languages like Haskell and Lisp, and features like Lambda functions in Python/C++.</p>
library|<div class='def-intro'>A collection of non-volatile resources used by computer programs, often for software development.</div><h3>Difference from Framework:</h3><p><strong>You</strong> call a library (e.g., NumPy). A <strong>Framework</strong> calls you (e.g., React/Angular).</p>
linked list|<div class='def-intro'>A linear collection of data elements whose order is not given by their physical placement in memory.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><p>Each element (Node) contains <strong>Data</strong> and a <strong>Pointer</strong> to the next node.</p><h3>Complexity:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Insert: O(1)</span> <span class='tag'>Search: O(N)</span></div>
linux|<div class='def-intro'>A family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel.</div><h3>Distros:</h3><ul><li><strong>Ubuntu:</strong> User-friendly.</li><li><strong>CentOS/RHEL:</strong> Enterprise choice.</li><li><strong>Kali:</strong> Security testing.</li></ul><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Runs 100% of the world's Top 500 Supercomputers.</p>
logic gate|<div class='def-intro'>An idealized scenario of computation or physical electronic device implementing a Boolean function.</div><h3>Primary Gates:</h3><ul><li><strong>AND:</strong> 1 if both inputs are 1.</li><li><strong>OR:</strong> 1 if either input is 1.</li><li><strong>NOT:</strong> Inverts the input.</li><li><strong>NAND:</strong> "Not AND" (Universal gate).</li></ul>
machine learning|<div class='def-intro'>A field of study in artificial intelligence concerned with the development and study of statistical algorithms that can learn from data.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Supervised:</strong> Learning from labeled data.</li><li><strong>Unsupervised:</strong> Finding patterns in unlabeled data.</li><li><strong>Reinforcement:</strong> Learning by reward/penalty.</li></ul>
macro|<div class='def-intro'>In computer science, a macro is a rule or pattern that specifies how a certain input should be mapped to a replacement output.</div><h3>Danger:</h3><p>Can lead to bugs since they are simple text replacements before compilation.</p><h3>Usage:</h3><p>#define MAX 100 in C++.</p>
magnetism|<div class='def-intro'>A class of physical phenomena that are mediated by magnetic fields.</div><h3>Core Concept:</h3><p>Like poles repel; Opposite poles attract.</p><h3>Real-world:</h3><p>Earth is a giant magnet, allowing us to use compasses.</p>
map|<div class='def-intro'>An abstract data type composed of a collection of (key, value) pairs, such that each possible key appears at most once.</div><h3>Implementation:</h3><ul><li><strong>std::map:</strong> Balanced BST (O(log N)).</li><li><strong>std::unordered_map:</strong> Hash Table (O(1)).</li></ul>
mars|<div class='def-intro'>The fourth planet from the Sun and the second-smallest planet in the Solar System.</div><h3>Key Facts:</h3><ul><li><strong>Color:</strong> Red due to iron oxide (rust).</li><li><strong>Atmosphere:</strong> Thin, mostly CO2.</li><li><strong>Mission:</strong> Perseverance rover is currently searching for signs of ancient life.</li></ul>
mathematics|<div class='def-intro'>The area of knowledge that includes the study of such topics as numbers, formulas and related structures, shapes and spaces.</div><h3>Role in CS:</h3><p>Linear Algebra for Graphics, Probability for ML, Number Theory for Encryption.</p>
matrix|<div class='def-intro'>A rectangular array or table of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns.</div><h3>Usage:</h3><p>Representing transformations in 3D games or neural network weights in AI.</p>
memory|<div class='def-intro'>The computer hardware devices used to store information for immediate use in a computer.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Volatile:</strong> Lost on power off (RAM).</li><li><strong>Non-Volatile:</strong> Persistent (SSD, Hard Drive).</li></ul>
merge sort|<div class='def-intro'>A divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that works by recursively splitting an array into halves and merging them in order.</div><h3>Complexity:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Time: O(N log N)</span> <span class='tag'>Space: O(N)</span></div><h3>Advantage:</h3><p>Guaranteed performance and stability (preserves order of equals).</p>
method|<div class='def-intro'>A procedure associated with a message and an object in object-oriented programming.</div><h3>Syntax:</h3><p>object.methodName(args)</p><h3>Purpose:</h3><p>Encapsulates behavior within an object.</p>
microservice|<div class='def-intro'>An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of services that are highly maintainable and testable.</div><h3>Pros:</h3><ul><li><strong>Agility:</strong> Teams can deploy independently.</li><li><strong>Scalability:</strong> Scale only the parts that need it.</li></ul><h3>Cons:</h3><p>Extreme operational complexity.</p>
mongodb|<div class='def-intro'>A source-available cross-platform document-oriented database program. Classified as a NoSQL database.</div><h3>Format:</h3><p>Uses JSON-like documents with optional schemas (BSON).</p><h3>Scaling:</h3><p>Designed for horizontal scaling through Sharding.</p>
moon|<div class='def-intro'>Earth's only natural satellite. It is the fifth-largest satellite in the Solar System.</div><h3>Discovery:</h3><p>Man first landed on the moon in 1969 (Apollo 11).</p><h3>Influence:</h3><p>Its gravitational pull causes the tides in our oceans.</p>
motherboard|<div class='def-intro'>The main printed circuit board (PCB) in general-purpose computers and other expandable systems.</div><h3>Components:</h3><p>CPU Socket, RAM slots, PCI-E lanes, Chipset, Southbridge.</p>
mysql|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) based on SQL.</div><h3>Popularity:</h3><p>The "M" in the LAMP stack (Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP).</p><h3>Owner:</h3><p>Oracle Corporation.</p>
namespace|<div class='def-intro'>A declarative region that provides a scope to the identifiers (the names of types, functions, variables, etc) inside it.</div><h3>Primary Goal:</h3><p>To avoid name collisions when using multiple libraries. For example, std::cout specifies the cout from the standard namespace.</p><h3>Usage (C++):</h3><p>using namespace std; or 
amespace myCode { ... }.</p>
network|<div class='def-intro'>A collection of computers, servers, mainframes, network devices, peripherals, or other devices connected to one another to allow the sharing of data.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>LAN:</strong> Local Area Network (Home/Office).</li><li><strong>WAN:</strong> Wide Area Network (The Internet).</li><li><strong>VPN:</strong> Virtual Private Network (Secure tunnel).</li></ul><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Enabled the digital age and global communication.</p>
neural network|<div class='def-intro'>A series of algorithms that endeavors to recognize underlying relationships in a set of data through a process that mimics the way the human brain operates.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><ul><li><strong>Input Layer:</strong> Receives the original data.</li><li><strong>Hidden Layers:</strong> Perform mathematical transformations.</li><li><strong>Output Layer:</strong> Provides the final prediction.</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>Conceived in the 1940s, but became powerful in the 2010s due to GPU acceleration and Big Data.</p>
neutron|<div class='def-intro'>A subatomic particle that has no charge and that is found in the nucleus of an atom.</div><h3>Properties:</h3><ul><li><strong>Mass:</strong> Slightly larger than a proton.</li><li><strong>Discovery:</strong> James Chadwick in 1932.</li></ul><h3>Importance:</h3><p>Maintains nuclear stability; without neutrons, positive protons would repel each other and the nucleus would explode.</p>
newton|<div class='def-intro'>Sir Isaac Newton (1642–1727). An English mathematician, physicist, astronomer, and author.</div><h3>Major Contributions:</h3><ul><li><strong>Laws of Motion:</strong> Inerita, Force=MA, Action/Reaction.</li><li><strong>Gravity:</strong> Theory of universal gravitation.</li><li><strong>Calculus:</strong> Developed independently alongside Leibniz.</li></ul>
node.js|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source, cross-platform, JavaScript runtime environment that executes JavaScript code outside a web browser.</div><h3>Key Feature:</h3><p><strong>Non-blocking I/O.</strong> It uses an event-driven architecture capable of asynchronous I/O.</p><h3>V8 Engine:</h3><p>Built on the same engine that powers Google Chrome, making it incredibly fast.</p><h3>Common Uses:</h3><p>Real-time chat apps, Streaming, API Backends.</p>
nosql|<div class='def-intro'>Stands for "Not Only SQL". A type of database that stores data in a format other than relational tables.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Document:</strong> MongoDB, CouchDB.</li><li><strong>Key-Value:</strong> Redis, DynamoDB.</li><li><strong>Column:</strong> Cassandra.</li><li><strong>Graph:</strong> Neo4j.</li></ul><h3>Pros:</h3><p>Flexible schemas, easy scaling (Horizontal).</p>
np-complete|<div class='def-intro'>The hardest problems in the NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial) complexity class.</div><h3>Definition:</h3><p>A problem is NP-Complete if it is in NP and every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.</p><h3>The P vs NP Problem:</h3><p>If anyone finds a polynomial-time solution for <strong>one</strong> NP-complete problem, all NP problems are solvable in record time ( Million prize available).</p>
null|<div class='def-intro'>A special marker used in computer programming to indicate that a data object does not exist.</div><h3>The Mistake:</h3><p>Tony Hoare, the inventor of Null, called it his "Billion Dollar Mistake" due to the endless crashes (NullPointerExceptions) it caused.</p>
object|<div class='def-intro'>A member (also called an instance) of a Java/C++ class. Each object has an identity, a behavior and a state.</div><h3>Philosophy:</h3><p>Objects allow us to model software after real-world things (e.g., a Car object has a color and a drive() method).</p>
object-oriented programming|<div class='def-intro'>A programming paradigm based on the concept of "objects", which can contain data and code.</div><h3>The 4 Pillars:</h3><ol><li><strong>Encapsulation:</strong> Hiding data.</li><li><strong>Abstraction:</strong> Showing essentials.</li><li><strong>Inheritance:</strong> Reusing code.</li><li><strong>Polymorphism:</strong> Many forms.</li></ol>
operating system|<div class='def-intro'>System software that manages computer hardware, software resources, and provides common services for computer programs.</div><h3>Market Share:</h3><ul><li><strong>Desktop:</strong> Windows (~70%), macOS (~15%).</li><li><strong>Mobile:</strong> Android (~70%), iOS (~28%).</li><li><strong>Servers:</strong> Linux (>90%).</li></ul>
overflow|<div class='def-intro'>In computing, overflow occurs when a calculation produces a result that exceeds the storage capacity of the intended data type.</div><h3>Risk:</h3><p>Can lead to security vulnerabilities (Buffer Overflow) or logical errors (Integer Overflow where numbers become negative suddenly).</p>
parameter|<div class='def-intro'>A special kind of variable used in a function to refer to one of the pieces of data provided as input to the subprogram.</div><h3>Vs Argument:</h3><p>Parameters are used in the function <strong>definition</strong>, Arguments are the actual values passed during the <strong>call</strong>.</p>
photosynthesis|<div class='def-intro'>The process by which green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods from carbon dioxide and water.</div><h3>Equation:</h3><p>6CO2 + 6H2O + Light Energy -> C6H12O6 + 6O2.</p><h3>Significance:</h3><p>The primary source of oxygen and energy for almost all life on Earth.</p>
php|<div class='def-intro'>A popular general-purpose scripting language that is especially suited to web development.</div><h3>History:</h3><p>Stands for "Hypertext Preprocessor". It was the foundation of Facebook, Wikipedia, and WordPress.</p><h3>Current State:</h3><p>Still powers over 75% of the web despite competition from newer languages.</p>
physics|<div class='def-intro'>The natural science that studies matter, its fundamental constituents, its motion and behavior through space and time.</div><h3>Main Goals:</h3><p>To understand how the universe behaves, from the smallest atoms to the largest galaxies.</p>
pixel|<div class='def-intro'>A minute area of illumination on a display screen, one of many from which an image is composed.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><p>Each pixel usually consists of sub-pixels in Red, Green, and Blue (RGB).</p><h3>Resolution:</h3><p>4K resolution means there are roughly 8 million pixels on the screen.</p>
pointer|<div class='def-intro'>A variable whose value is the address of another variable.</div><h3>The Strength of C++:</h3><p>Allows for low-level memory control, which is why C++ is used for game engines and high-performance trading systems.</p><h3>The Danger:</h3><p>Incorrect use leads to memory leaks and "Segmentation Faults".</p>
polymorphism|<div class='def-intro'>The ability of an object to take on many forms.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>Static:</strong> Function Overloading / Templates (Compile-time).</li><li><strong>Dynamic:</strong> Method Overriding with Virtual functions (Runtime).</li></ul><h3>Benefit:</h3><p>Allows you to treat different objects in a uniform way.</p>
polynomial time|<div class='def-intro'>In complexity theory, polynomial time refers to an algorithm whose execution time is bounded by a polynomial expression.</div><h3>Notation:</h3><p>O(N^k) where k is a constant. This is the criteria for an algorithm to be considered "Efficient".</p>
postgresql|<div class='def-intro'>A powerful, open-source object-relational database system with over 30 years of active development.</div><h3>Claim to Fame:</h3><p>Extremely reliable, handles complex data types, and is ACID-compliant by default.</p>
priority queue|<div class='def-intro'>An abstract data type similar to a regular queue or stack data structure in which each element additionally has a "priority".</div><h3>Logic:</h3><p>An element with high priority is served before an element with low priority.</p><h3>Implementation:</h3><p>Usually implemented using a <strong>Binary Heap</strong>.</p>
process|<div class='def-intro'>The instance of a computer program that is being executed by one or many threads.</div><h3>Isolation:</h3><p>A process cannot access the memory of another process directly (unlike threads), which makes them more secure and stable.</p>
programming|<div class='def-intro'>The process of creating a set of instructions that tell a computer how to perform a task.</div><h3>Evolution:</h3><p>Punch Cards -> Assembly -> Procedural (C) -> OOP (Java) -> Functional/Modern (Rust/Go).</p>
protocol|<div class='def-intro'>A set of rules or procedures for transmitting data between electronic devices.</div><h3>Common Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>HTTP:</strong> Web browsing.</li><li><strong>SMTP:</strong> Email.</li><li><strong>FTP:</strong> File transfer.</li><li><strong>TCP:</strong> Reliable data transfer.</li></ul>
proton|<div class='def-intro'>A subatomic particle, symbol p, with a positive electric charge.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><p>Made of smaller particles called <strong>Quarks</strong> (Two Ups, One Down).</p><h3>Significance:</h3><p>The number of protons determines what element an atom is (Atomic Number).</p>
python|<div class='def-intro'>A high-level, general-purpose programming language known for its readability.</div><h3>Strengths:</h3><ul><li><strong>Simplicity:</strong> Clean syntax that looks like English.</li><li><strong>Libraries:</strong> NumPy, PyTorch, Django, Flask.</li><li><strong>Community:</strong> Huge support for AI and Data Science.</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>Created by Guido van Rossum and released in 1991.</p>
quantum computing|<div class='def-intro'>A type of computation whose operations can harness the phenomena of quantum mechanics.</div><h3>Qubits:</h3><p>Unlike bits (0 or 1), Qubits can exist in a state of <strong>Superposition</strong> (both 0 and 1 at the same time).</p><h3>Promise:</h3><p>Could solve problems in minutes that would take today's supercomputers 10,000 years.</p>
queue|<div class='def-intro'>A linear data structure which follows a particular order in which the operations are performed.</div><h3>Principle:</h3><p><strong>FIFO (First In, First Out).</strong> Like a line at a movie theater.</p><h3>Ops:</h3><ul><li><strong>Enqueue:</strong> Add to back.</li><li><strong>Dequeue:</strong> Remove from front.</li></ul>
quick sort|<div class='def-intro'>An efficient, recursive, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm.</div><h3>Strategy:</h3><ol><li>Pick a <strong>Pivot</strong>.</li><li>Partition the array (Smaller to left, larger to right).</li><li>Sort the halves recursively.</li></ol><h3>Performance:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Avg: O(N log N)</span> <span class='tag'>Worst: O(N^2)</span></div>
ram|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Random Access Memory</strong>. A type of data storage used in computers that is generally located on the motherboard.</div><h3>Characteristics:</h3><ul><li><strong>Volatile:</strong> Loses data when powered off.</li><li><strong>Speed:</strong> 100x faster than a typical SSD/Hard Drive.</li></ul>
react|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source front-end JavaScript library for building user interfaces based on components.</div><h3>Innovation:</h3><ul><li><strong>Virtual DOM:</strong> Only updates parts of the page that changed.</li><li><strong>JSX:</strong> Writing HTML-like code inside JavaScript.</li><li><strong>Components:</strong> Reusable UI blocks.</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>Developed by Jordan Walke at Facebook and released in 2013.</p>
recursion|<div class='def-intro'>A technique where the solution to a problem depends on solutions to smaller instances of the same problem.</div><h3>The Rule:</h3><ol><li><strong>Base Case:</strong> When to stop.</li><li><strong>Recursive Step:</strong> Call itself with simpler input.</li></ol><h3>Classic Example:</h3><p>Calculating Factorial: act(n) = n * fact(n-1).</p>
redis|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Remote Dictionary Server</strong>. An open-source, in-memory data structure store, used as a database, cache, and message broker.</div><h3>Speed:</h3><p>Capable of performing millions of operations per second with microsecond latency.</p><h3>Data types:</h3><p>Strings, Hashes, Lists, Sets, Geospatial indexes.</p>
regular expression|<div class='def-intro'>A sequence of characters that specifies a search pattern in text.</div><h3>Common Symbols:</h3><ul><li><strong>.:</strong> Matches any character.</li><li><strong>*:</strong> Matches zero or more.</li><li><strong>^/ $:</strong> Start and end of line.</li></ul><h3>Uses:</h3><p>Email validation, Web scraping, Complex find-and-replace.</p>
relational database|<div class='def-intro'>A type of database that stores and provides access to data points that are related to one another.</div><h3>Schema:</h3><p>Data is organized in <strong>Tables</strong> (Rows and Columns).</p><h3>Standard:</h3><p><strong>SQL</strong> (Structured Query Language) is the language used to interact with them.</p>
rest|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Representational State Transfer</strong>. An architectural style for providing standards between computer systems on the web.</div><h3>Principles:</h3><ul><li><strong>Stateless:</strong> Server doesn't remember previous requests.</li><li><strong>Uniform Interface:</strong> Consistent URIs (Ex: /users/1).</li><li><strong>Verbs:</strong> GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.</li></ul>
robotics|<div class='def-intro'>An interdisciplinary branch of computer science and engineering.</div><h3>Goal:</h3><p>To design machines (robots) that can assist humans or perform tasks humans cannot do.</p><h3>The 3 Laws (Asimov):</h3><p>1. Don't hurt humans. 2. Obey orders. 3. Protect own existence (if not conflicting with 1 or 2).</p>
router|<div class='def-intro'>A networking device that forwards data packets between computer networks.</div><h3>Function:</h3><p>Connects your home network to the Internet Service Provider (ISP). It uses an <strong>IP Table</strong> to decide which way to send data.</p>
rust|<div class='def-intro'>A multi-paradigm, high-level, general-purpose programming language designed for performance and safety.</div><h3>The Innovation:</h3><p><strong>Ownership System.</strong> Rust guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector by tracking who owns what data in memory at compile-time.</p><h3>Popularity:</h3><p>Voted "Most Loved Language" in the Stack Overflow Survey for 8 years in a row.</p>
saturn|<div class='def-intro'>The sixth planet from the Sun and the second-largest in the Solar System.</div><h3>Key Feature:</h3><p>Its spectacular <strong>Rings</strong>, which are composed mostly of ice particles with a smaller amount of rocky debris and dust.</p><h3>Density:</h3><p>Saturn is less dense than water; if you had a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float!</p>
science|<div class='def-intro'>A systematic enterprise that builds and organizes knowledge in the form of testable explanations and predictions about the universe.</div><h3>Categories:</h3><ul><li><strong>Natural:</strong> Biology, Physics, Chemistry.</li><li><strong>Social:</strong> Psychology, Sociology.</li><li><strong>Formal:</strong> Logic, Mathematics.</li></ul>
scripting language|<div class='def-intro'>A programming language that is used to manipulate, customize, and automate the facilities of an existing system.</div><h3>Examples:</h3><p>Python, Bash, PowerShell, Lua (used in games like Roblox/World of Warcraft).</p>
search engine|<div class='def-intro'>A software system that is designed to carry out web searches.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><ol><li><strong>Crawling:</strong> Bots visit billions of pages.</li><li><strong>Indexing:</strong> Building a search-map of words.</li><li><strong>Ranking:</strong> Using algorithms (like PageRank) to show the best results first.</li></ol>
security|<div class='def-intro'>The state of being free from danger or threat. In computing, it's the protection of hardware, software, and data.</div><h3>The CIA Triad:</h3><ul><li><strong>Confidentiality:</strong> No unauthorized reading.</li><li><strong>Integrity:</strong> No unauthorized changing.</li><li><strong>Availability:</strong> Ready when needed.</li></ul>
semaphore|<div class='def-intro'>A variable used for controlling access to a common resource by multiple processes in a concurrent system.</div><h3>Metaphor:</h3><p>Like a locker key system. If all keys are gone (Counter=0), you must wait till one is returned.</p>
server|<div class='def-intro'>A computer program or device that provides a service to another computer program and its user, also known as the client.</div><h3>Hardware:</h3><p>Usually kept in high-security Climate-Controlled <strong>Data Centers</strong>.</p><h3>Role:</h3><p>Stores the code and files for websites like YouTube or Facebook.</p>
software|<div class='def-intro'>A collection of instructions and data that tell a computer how to work.</div><h3>Types:</h3><ul><li><strong>System Software:</strong> OS and drivers.</li><li><strong>Application Software:</strong> Chrome, Word, Fortnite.</li></ul><h3>History:</h3><p>The term was coined by John Tukey in 1958.</p>
solar system|<div class='def-intro'>The gravitationally bound system of the Sun and the objects that orbit it.</div><h3>Planets (In order):</h3><p>Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.</p><h3>Age:</h3><p>4.6 billion years.</p>
sorting algorithm|<div class='def-intro'>An algorithm that puts elements of a list in a certain order (numerical or lexicographical).</div><h3>Stability:</h3><p>A stable sort preserves the relative order of items with equal keys.</p><h3>Examples:</h3><p>Merge Sort (Stable), Quick Sort (Unstable), Heap Sort (Unstable).</p>
sql|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Structured Query Language</strong>. The standard language for managing relational databases.</div><h3>Key Statements:</h3><ul><li><strong>SELECT:</strong> Get data.</li><li><strong>JOIN:</strong> Combine data from two tables.</li><li><strong>DELETE:</strong> Remove data.</li></ul>
ssl|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Secure Sockets Layer</strong>. The standard security technology for establishing an encrypted link between a web server and a browser.</div><h3>Legacy:</h3><p>Technically succeeded by <strong>TLS (Transport Layer Security)</strong>, though most people still say "SSL".</p>
stack|<div class='def-intro'>A linear data structure which follows a particular order in which the operations are performed.</div><h3>Principle:</h3><p><strong>LIFO (Last In, First Out).</strong> Like a stack of plates.</p><h3>Operations:</h3><ul><li><strong>Push:</strong> Add to top.</li><li><strong>Pop:</strong> Remove from top.</li></ul>
string|<div class='def-intro'>In computer programming, a string is traditionally a sequence of characters, either as a literal constant or as some kind of variable.</div><h3>Encodings:</h3><ul><li><strong>ASCII:</strong> 128 characters.</li><li><strong>UTF-8:</strong> Supports all languages (Emojis included!).</li></ul>
structure|<div class='def-intro'>A user-defined data type in C/C++ that allows you to combine different data types under a single name.</div><h3>Memory Layout:</h3><p>Members are stored in contiguous memory locations in the order they were defined.</p>
swift|<div class='def-intro'>A general-purpose, multi-paradigm, compiled programming language developed by Apple Inc.</div><h3>Usage:</h3><p>The primary language for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS development.</p><h3>Safety:</h3><p>Designed to eliminate entire classes of common programming errors by using modern patterns.</p>
syntax|<div class='def-intro'>In computer science, the syntax of a computer language is the set of rules that defines the combinations of symbols that are considered to be correctly structured.</div><h3>Analogy:</h3><p>Syntax is the "grammar" of the programming language.</p>
tcp|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Transmission Control Protocol</strong>. A basic communication language of the internet.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Establishes a connection (3-way handshake) and ensures that all data packets arrive in the correct order without errors.</p><h3>Reliability:</h3><p>If a packet is lost, it is automatically re-sent. This makes TCP ideal for web browsing and file transfers.</p>
technology|<div class='def-intro'>The application of scientific knowledge for practical purposes, especially in industry.</div><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Has transformed human society through eras like the Industrial Revolution, the Space Age, and the Information Age.</p><h3>Current Trend:</h3><p>Convergence of Biotech, Nanotech, and AI.</p>
thread|<div class='def-intro'>The smallest sequence of programmed instructions that can be managed independently by a scheduler.</div><h3>Multithreading:</h3><p>Executing multiple threads at once to speed up a program (e.g., one thread for UI, one for networking).</p><h3>Risk:</h3><p><strong>Race Conditions.</strong> When two threads try to change the same data simultaneously.</p>
tree|<div class='def-intro'>A widely used abstract data type that simulates a hierarchical tree structure, with a root value and subtrees of children with a parent node.</div><h3>Key Terms:</h3><ul><li><strong>Root:</strong> The top node.</li><li><strong>Leaf:</strong> A node with no children.</li><li><strong>Depth:</strong> Distance from the root.</li></ul><h3>Applications:</h3><p>HTML DOM, Folder structures, Decision trees in ML.</p>
typescript|<div class='def-intro'>A programming language developed by Microsoft that builds on JavaScript by adding static type definitions.</div><h3>Benefit:</h3><p>Catch errors during development instead of when the app is running. It's "JavaScript that scales."</p><h3>Compatibility:</h3><p>TS compiles into plain JS, so it runs anywhere JS runs.</p>
udp|<div class='def-intro'><strong>User Datagram Protocol</strong>. A communication protocol used across the Internet for especially time-sensitive transmissions.</div><h3>Pros:</h3><ul><li><strong>Speed:</strong> No handshake or order-checking.</li><li><strong>Low Latency:</strong> Perfect for streaming and gaming.</li></ul><h3>Cons:</h3><p><strong>Unreliable.</strong> Some data might be lost or arrive out of order.</p>
unix|<div class='def-intro'>A family of multitasking, multiuser computer operating systems that derive from the original AT&T Unix, developed in the 1970s.</div><h3>Philosophy:</h3><p>"Write programs that do one thing and do it well. Write programs to work together."</p><h3>Mascot:</h3><p>Dennis Ritchie and Ken Thompson are the legends behind it.</p>
url|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Uniform Resource Locator</strong>. Colloquially known as a web address.</div><h3>Anatomy:</h3><p>https://www.google.com/search -> Protocol, Host, Path.</p><h3>Purpose:</h3><p>Provides a globally unique address for any resource on the web.</p>
variable|<div class='def-intro'>A value that can change depending on conditions or on information passed to the program.</div><h3>Naming:</h3><p>In most languages, names must start with a letter and have no spaces.</p><h3>Scope:</h3><ul><li><strong>Global:</strong> Visible everywhere.</li><li><strong>Local:</strong> Visible only within a block or function.</li></ul>
vector|<div class='def-intro'>A sequence container that encapsulates dynamic size arrays.</div><h3>How it works:</h3><p>Like an array, but if it runs out of space, it automatically copies itself into a new, larger memory block (usually double the size).</p><h3>Complexity:</h3><div class='complexity-box'><span class='tag'>Push Back: O(1) Avg</span> <span class='tag'>Access: O(1)</span></div>
virtual machine|<div class='def-intro'>The virtualization/emulation of a computer system. VMs are based on computer architectures and provide functionality of a physical computer.</div><h3>Uses:</h3><ul><li><strong>Testing:</strong> Run Linux on a Windows PC.</li><li><strong>Cloud:</strong> AWS EC2 instances are virtual machines.</li><li><strong>Security:</strong> Isolating malware for analysis.</li></ul>
virtual reality|<div class='def-intro'>A simulated experience that can be similar to or completely different from the real world.</div><h3>Hardware:</h3><p>Headsets like Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro, and Valve Index.</p><h3>Impact:</h3><p>Revolutionizing gaming, surgeon training, and industrial design.</p>
virus|<div class='def-intro'>A type of computer program that, when executed, replicates itself by modifying other computer programs and inserting its own code.</div><h3>History:</h3><p>The first "creeper" virus was created in the 70s on ARPANET.</p><h3>Protection:</h3><p><strong>Antivirus software</strong> uses signatures or heuristics to detect and kill them.</p>
visual studio code|<div class='def-intro'>A free source-code editor made by Microsoft. It is currently the most popular developer tool in the world.</div><h3>Winning Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Extensions:</strong> Thousands of plugins.</li><li><strong>IntelliSense:</strong> Smart code completion.</li><li><strong>Integrated Terminal:</strong> Run code inside the editor.</li></ul>
vue.js|<div class='def-intro'>An open-source model–view–viewmodel JavaScript framework for building user interfaces and single-page applications.</div><h3>Philosophy:</h3><p>Designed to be incrementally adoptable. You can use a little bit of it or the whole thing.</p><h3>Pros:</h3><p>Extremely lightweight and easy to learn compared to Angular.</p>
web|<div class='def-intro'>Common shorthand for the World Wide Web.</div><h3>Structure:</h3><p>Based on <strong>Hyperlinks</strong> that connect documents across the globe via URLs.</p>
wifi|<div class='def-intro'>A family of wireless network protocols based on the IEEE 802.11 standards.</div><h3>Frequency:</h3><p>Operates at 2.4 GHz (Longer range) or 5 GHz (Faster speed).</p><h3>Security:</h3><p>Always use <strong>WPA3</strong> encryption to protect from hacks.</p>
windows|<div class='def-intro'>A group of several proprietary graphical operating system families developed and marketed by Microsoft.</div><h3>Dominance:</h3><p>Powers over 1.4 billion PCs. It remains the standard for gaming and corporate enterprise work.</p>
word|<div class='def-intro'>In computing, a word is the natural unit of data used by a particular processor design.</div><h3>Sizes:</h3><ul><li><strong>32-bit:</strong> Max 4GB RAM.</li><li><strong>64-bit:</strong> Modern standard, can handle nearly infinite RAM.</li></ul>
world wide web|<div class='def-intro'>An information system on the Internet that allows documents to be connected to other documents by hypertext links.</div><h3>Inventor:</h3><p><strong>Tim Berners-Lee</strong> at CERN in 1989. He famously did not patent it, keeping it free for the world.</p>
xml|<div class='def-intro'><strong>Extensible Markup Language</strong>. A markup language and file format for storing, transmitting, and reconstructing arbitrary data.</div><h3>Vs JSON:</h3><p>Both do the same thing, but XML is more "wordy" and supports custom tags and validation (schemas).</p>
youtube|<div class='def-intro'>A global online video sharing and social media platform headquartered in San Bruno, California.</div><h3>Scale:</h3><p>Users upload over 500 hours of video every single minute. It is the second most visited website in the world after Google.</p>
zip|<div class='def-intro'>An archive file format that supports lossless data compression.</div><h3>Algorithm:</h3><p>Typically uses the <strong>DEFLATE</strong> algorithm to shrink file sizes by finding patterns.</p>
zoom|<div class='def-intro'>A communications technology company that provides videotelephony and online chat services.</div><h3>Cultural Impact:</h3><p>Became a "household name" during the 2020 pandemic as the primary tool for remote work and education.</p>
photosynthesis|<div class='def-intro'>The process by which plants convert light into energy.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Chlorophyll captures sunlight, converting CO2 and water into glucose and oxygen.</p>
gravity|<div class='def-intro'>The force of attraction between masses.</div><h3>Mechanism:</h3><p>Curvature of spacetime by mass (General Relativity).</p>
nucleus|<div class='def-intro'>The control center of a cell.</div><h3>Function:</h3><p>Contains genetic material and orchestrates growth and reproduction.</p>
mona lisa|<div class='def-intro'>A half-length portrait painting by Italian artist Leonardo da Vinci. It is the best-known and most-visited work of art in the world.</div><h3>Key Features:</h3><ul><li><strong>Enigmatic Smile:</strong> Subject of endless analysis.</li><li><strong>Sfumato:</strong> A technique of blending colors to create soft transitions.</li><li><strong>History:</strong> Stolen in 1911, which skyrocketed its fame.</li></ul><h3>Location:</h3><p>Louvre Museum, Paris.</p>
zeus|<div class='def-intro'>The sky and thunder god in ancient Greek religion, who ruled as king of the gods of Mount Olympus.</div><h3>Symbols:</h3><ul><li><strong>Thunderbolt:</strong> His primary weapon.</li><li><strong>Eagle:</strong> His sacred animal.</li><li><strong>Oak Tree:</strong> His sacred plant.</li></ul><h3>Mythology:</h3><p>Defeated the Titans and led the new generation of gods.</p>
football|<div class='def-intro'>Also known as Soccer. A team sport played with a spherical ball between two teams of eleven players.</div><h3>Tournament:</h3><p>The <strong>FIFA World Cup</strong> is the most watched sporting event on the planet.</p><h3>Key Figures:</h3><p>Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Pelé, Diego Maradona.</p>
basketball|<div class='def-intro'>A game played between two teams of five players in which goals are scored by throwing a ball through a netted hoop.</div><h3>Global League:</h3><p>The <strong>NBA</strong> (National Basketball Association) is the premier professional league.</p><h3>Skills:</h3><p>Dribbling, Shooting (3-pointers), Dunks, Defensive blocks.</p>
galaxy cluster|<div class='def-intro'>A structure that consists of anywhere from hundreds to thousands of galaxies that is bound together by gravity.</div><h3>Observation:</h3><p>Studied using the Hubble and James Webb Space Telescopes to understand the early universe.</p>
black hole singularity|<div class='def-intro'>The very center of a black hole where all the mass is crushed into an infinitely small, infinitely dense point.</div><h3>Physics:</h3><p>Space and time as we know it cease to exist here, challenging all known laws of physics.</p>
industrial revolution|<div class='def-intro'>The transition to new manufacturing processes in Europe and the US, in the period from about 1760 to 1840.</div><h3>Innovations:</h3><ul><li><strong>Steam Engine:</strong> Powered factories and trains.</li><li><strong>Spinning Jenny:</strong> Revolutionized textiles.</li><li><strong>Telegraph:</strong> Instant communication over distance.</li></ul>
renaissance|<div class='def-intro'>A fervent period of European cultural, artistic, political and economic "rebirth" following the Middle Ages.</div><h3>Legends:</h3><p>Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael, Donatello.</p>
